target.title: Make Telegram sendMessage actions durable
target.body:
## Summary

- Routes Telegram `sendMessage` action delivery through the durable outbound final-send path instead of direct Telegram platform sends.
- Preserves Telegram action payload features on the durable path, including buttons, quote/reply options, media/voice options, session context, gateway scopes, and delivery pin requests.
- Extends delivery pin scope propagation through the core outbound adapter seam and adds queue-level retry/ack proof.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-message.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` passed: 4 files, 180 tests.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound.ts src/channels/plugins/outbound.types.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts` passed.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts` passed.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` passed.
- `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 /Users/marianobelinky/agent-shared/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` passed with no accepted/actionable findings.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram `sendMessage` actions now enqueue durable final deliveries before the Telegram platform send, so a slow or timed-out gateway/platform send leaves retryable state instead of losing the completed agent output.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout using the real durable outbound queue code with a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`; Telegram platform calls were fault-injected in the focused action-runtime test.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-message.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`.
Evidence after fix: The new action-runtime test observes the queue entry on disk before the Telegram send runs, verifies timeout leaves a pending retryable entry with `retryCount: 1`, and verifies a later successful durable send is acked/removed.
Observed result after fix: Focused tests passed, including retry persistence, successful ack, delivery pin preservation, and gateway scope propagation into Telegram pinning.
What was not tested: Live Telegram delivery against a real bot was not run in this PR; this is covered with local queue/platform fault injection only.

target.title: fix(doctor): auto-repair stale session snapshot paths on --fix
target.body:
## Summary

Add `shouldRepair` support to `noteSessionSnapshotHealth()` so that `openclaw doctor --fix` can rewrite stale session snapshot paths in `sessions.json` instead of only reporting them as warnings.

Fixes #85689

## Problem

After a pnpm update changes the runtime root (e.g. `openclaw@2026.5.20_@types+express@5.0.6` → `openclaw@2026.5.20`), sessions that cached `skillsSnapshot.prompt` paths referencing the old root become stale. `openclaw doctor` correctly detects this and reports findings with both `cachedPath` and `expectedPath`, but:

1. `noteSessionSnapshotHealth()` has no `shouldRepair` parameter — it's always report-only
2. The update flow runs `doctor --fix` post-update, but session snapshot paths are never repaired
3. Users must manually edit `sessions.json` or reset affected sessions (losing history)

## Fix

The scan logic (`scanSessionStoreForStaleRuntimeSnapshotPaths`) already computes both `cachedPath` and `expectedPath` for every finding. The repair replaces stale paths in the raw JSON text:

1. Add `shouldRepair?: boolean` to `noteSessionSnapshotHealth()` params
2. When `shouldRepair=true`: replace stale path prefixes with live bundled skills root
3. Handle JSON-escaped paths (Windows backslashes, unicode) via `JSON.stringify().slice(1,-1)`
4. Handle XML-escaped paths in `skillsSnapshot.prompt` (`&`, `<`, `>`, `"`, `'`)
5. For `baseDir` paths in `resolvedSkills`, also replace the directory prefix without `SKILL.md` (both POSIX `/SKILL.md` and Windows `\\SKILL.md`)
6. Create `.bak` backup before writing (mode 0o600)
7. Use `writeTextAtomic` for session-store-safe writes (atomic, prevents partial writes)
8. Pass `ctx.prompter.shouldRepair` from `runSessionSnapshotsHealth()`

This follows the same pattern as other doctor repair functions (`noteSessionTranscriptHealth`, `maybeRepairLegacyCronStore`, `maybeScrubConfigAuditLog`).

## Changes

- `src/commands/doctor-session-snapshots.ts`: +100 lines (repair logic with JSON/XML-escaped path handling, atomic writes)
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`: +1 line (pass shouldRepair)
- `repro-doctor-session-snapshot-repair.mjs`: standalone proof script (5 scenarios)

## Path Replacement Strategy

The repair handles three forms of stale paths in sessions.json:

1. **JSON-escaped** (Windows backslashes, unicode): `JSON.stringify().slice(1,-1)` generates the escaped form
2. **XML-escaped** (skillsSnapshot.prompt locations): `&` `<` `>` `"` `'` entity encoding
3. **Raw unescaped** (fallback for non-encoded storage)

For `resolvedSkills[].baseDir` paths, the repair also replaces the directory prefix (without the trailing `SKILL.md` segment), handling both POSIX (`/SKILL.md`) and Windows (`\\SKILL.md`) separators.

All writes use `writeTextAtomic` from `../infra/json-files.js` to prevent partial writes or corruption.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw doctor --fix` reports stale session snapshot paths but never repairs them. After a pnpm runtime root change, sessions.json caches paths that no longer exist. The scan already computes the correct target path, but the report-only note function discards it. The repair must handle JSON-escaped paths (Windows backslashes), XML-escaped paths (`&` in skillsSnapshot.prompt), and baseDir directory paths in resolvedSkills (both POSIX and Windows separators).

**Real environment tested:** Linux x64, Node v24.14.0. Live production instance with 14 sessions affected by pnpm runtime root change (see Issue #85689 for live output). Standalone reproduction script (`repro-doctor-session-snapshot-repair.mjs`) mirrors the exact repair logic from the PR.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
node repro-doctor-session-snapshot-repair.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:**

**1. Live production instance (from Issue #85689):**

```
Session snapshots
- Found 14 sessions with stale cached session metadata paths.
  Live bundled skills root is healthy:
  ~/.local/share/pnpm/global/5/.pnpm/openclaw@2026.5.20/...
  Cached session metadata still references an inactive runtime
  root; start a fresh session or reset the affected long-lived
  sessions after confirming history can be retired.
  Store: ~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  - agent:main:feishu:direct:ou_bc71ec62b088ed55e66fcc23c18a0056
    skillsSnapshot.prompt:
    ~/.local/share/pnpm/global/5/.pnpm/openclaw@2026.5.20_@ty
  pes+express@5.0.6/.../clawhub/SKILL.md
    ->
    ~/.local/share/pnpm/global/5/.pnpm/openclaw@2026.5.20/...
  nclaw/skills/clawhub/SKILL.md
  ...and 209 more stale cached paths.
```

**2. Standalone reproduction script output (5 scenarios):**

```
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Real Behavior Proof: Doctor Session Snapshot Auto-Repair
Date: 2026-05-27T05:45:52.327Z
Node: v24.14.0 | Platform: linux x64
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scenario 1: WITHOUT repair — findings computed but not applied
  Findings computed: 3 stale paths
  Repair performed: NO (shouldRepair not enabled)
  Stale paths remaining: 3
  PASS: findings detected, no repair

Scenario 2: WITH repair — paths replaced correctly
  Paths replaced: 3
  Backup created: true
  JSON valid: true
  Sessions preserved: 2
  Stale paths remaining: 0
  PASS: repair applied, JSON valid, backup created, no stale paths

Scenario 3: Windows backslash paths — JSON-escaped in file
  Input: Windows path with backslashes
  JSON-escaped form: C:\\Users\\user\\.local\\share\\pnpm\\gl...
  Repaired path contains live root: true
  Repaired path contains stale root: false
  PASS: Windows backslash paths repaired correctly

Scenario 4: XML-escaped prompt paths — entity-encoded in file
  Input: Path with & character (XML-escaped as &)
  XML-escaped stale root: /home/user/.local/share/pnpm/global/5/node_modules...
  Repaired contains live root: true
  Repaired contains stale root: false
  PASS: XML-escaped prompt paths repaired correctly

Scenario 5: Idempotent — second run finds nothing
  Second scan findings: 0
  PASS: no stale paths after repair

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SUMMARY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  Without repair: findings computed, stale paths persist
  With repair:    paths replaced, JSON valid, backup created
  Windows paths:  JSON-escaped backslash paths handled correctly
  XML-escaped:    & entity paths in prompt handled correctly
  Idempotent:     second scan finds nothing to repair

  Results: 5 passed, 0 failed

  The repair handles three path forms:
  1. JSON-escaped (Windows backslashes, unicode)
  2. XML-escaped (& < > in skillsSnapshot.prompt)
  3. Raw unescaped (fallback for non-encoded storage)
  All forms are replaced with the live bundled skills root.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

**Observed result after fix:** All 5 scenarios pass. The live production instance (Issue #85689) shows the exact real-world scenario this PR addresses: 14 sessions with 223 stale paths after pnpm runtime root change. The repair correctly handles POSIX paths, Windows backslash paths (JSON-escaped), XML-escaped entity paths in skillsSnapshot.prompt, and baseDir directory paths (both POSIX `/SKILL.md` and Windows `\\SKILL.md`). The repair is idempotent (Scenario 5). JSON validity is preserved after rewriting. Writes use `writeTextAtomic` for session-store-safe atomic operations.

**What was not tested:**
- Live `openclaw doctor --fix` on the affected instance (requires building from PR branch — local build fails due to missing native bindings in container environment, documented in AGENTS.md)
- Concurrent access to sessions.json during repair (single-threaded doctor execution)

**Proof coverage:**
The standalone script (`repro-doctor-session-snapshot-repair.mjs`) mirrors the exact repair logic from `src/commands/doctor-session-snapshots.ts`:
- JSON.stringify().slice(1,-1) for JSON-escaped path generation
- XML entity escaping for `&` `<` `>` `"` `'`
- Try JSON-escaped form first, then XML-escaped, then raw as fallback
- Handles Windows backslashes, unicode escapes, special characters
- Backup creation before write
- JSON validity preservation after repair
- Uses `writeTextAtomic` for session-store-safe writes

target.title: fix(hooks): include media metadata in toPluginMessageReceivedEvent
target.body:
## Summary

ClawSweeper review identified that the original fix only included `mediaPath`/`mediaType`/`mediaPaths`/`mediaTypes` but omitted `mediaUrl` and `mediaUrls`, which are available in `FinalizedMsgContext` and needed by plugins handling URL-based media references.

**Key change**: Added `mediaUrl` and `mediaUrls` fields to `CanonicalInboundMessageHookContext`, `deriveInboundMessageHookContext`, `toPluginInboundClaimEvent`, and `toPluginMessageReceivedEvent`.

## Changes
- `src/hooks/message-hook-mappers.ts`: Add mediaUrl/mediaUrls to type, derive function, and both event mappers
- `src/hooks/message-hook-mappers.test.ts`: Add test coverage for all six media metadata fields

target.title: fix(config): skip .openclaw append when OPENCLAW_HOME already names a state dir (#45765)
target.body:
## Summary

Setting `OPENCLAW_HOME=~/.openclaw` produces the nested directory `~/.openclaw/.openclaw/` and onboarding writes the config file to the wrong place. Reporter on Linux Kylin V10 / aarch64 (and macOS 15.4) documented a 100% reproduction rate on OpenClaw 2026.3.12 (#45765). This change adds a closed-set nesting guard so that when `OPENCLAW_HOME` already names a known state directory (`.openclaw` or the legacy `.clawdbot`), the explicit home is used as the state/config directory directly instead of being treated as a parent that needs a `.openclaw` subdirectory.

## Root Cause

- `src/config/paths.ts:60-89` — `resolveStateDir()` calls `newStateDir(effectiveHomedir)` which unconditionally appends `NEW_STATE_DIRNAME = ".openclaw"` to whatever home dir comes back from `resolveRequiredHomeDir()`. When the operator explicitly set `OPENCLAW_HOME=~/.openclaw`, the resolved home is already `~/.openclaw`, so the helper produces `~/.openclaw/.openclaw`.
- `src/utils.ts:119-141` — `resolveConfigDir()` does the same: `path.join(resolveRequiredHomeDir(env, homedir), ".openclaw")`.
- `src/config/paths.ts:272-296` — `resolveDefaultConfigCandidates()` builds candidate config paths through the same `newStateDir(effectiveHomedir)` / `legacyStateDirs(effectiveHomedir)` helpers and emits the nested `~/.openclaw/.openclaw/openclaw.json` path as the canonical first candidate. Because `resolveConfigPathCandidate()` returns the first existing path from that list, onboarding writes the config to the shadow nested directory and every subsequent read picks it up too.
- Execution path: `openclaw onboard` → `resolveConfigPathCandidate` → `resolveDefaultConfigCandidates` → `newStateDir(effectiveHomedir)` → `path.join(~/.openclaw, ".openclaw")` → writes config to `~/.openclaw/.openclaw/openclaw.json`.

This follows clawsweeper's direction on #45765:
*"Add a narrow known-state-directory basename guard for explicit `OPENCLAW_HOME` in state and config resolution, preserve documented parent-home behavior, and cover `.openclaw` plus supported legacy state directory names with regression tests."*

## Changes

- `src/config/paths.ts`:
  - Export `ALL_STATE_DIRNAMES` (`ReadonlySet<string>`) combining `NEW_STATE_DIRNAME` (`.openclaw`) and `LEGACY_STATE_DIRNAMES` (`.clawdbot`). Any new legacy state-dir name added to `LEGACY_STATE_DIRNAMES` joins the guard automatically.
  - Export `isExplicitOpenClawHomeStateDir(env, resolvedHome)` — returns `true` only when `env.OPENCLAW_HOME?.trim()` is non-empty AND `path.basename(resolvedHome)` is in `ALL_STATE_DIRNAMES`. The implicit `$HOME` / `$USERPROFILE` path keeps the documented parent-home convention even when the OS home happens to be named `.openclaw`; we opt in only when the operator was explicit.
  - `resolveStateDir()`: if the guard matches, return the effective home directly (skip `newStateDir(effectiveHomedir)`).
  - `resolveDefaultConfigCandidates()`: if the guard matches, use `[home]` as the single default directory list instead of `[newStateDir, ...legacyStateDirs]`, so candidate paths become `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` (and the legacy filename variants under the same dir) instead of `~/.openclaw/.openclaw/openclaw.json`.
- `src/utils.ts`:
  - Import `isExplicitOpenClawHomeStateDir` from `./config/paths.js` (no circular dependency — `paths.ts` does not import from `utils.ts`).
  - `resolveConfigDir()`: if the guard matches, return the explicit home directly instead of `path.join(home, ".openclaw")`.
- `src/config/paths.test.ts`: add 6 new regression cases under a new `OPENCLAW_HOME nesting guard (#45765)` describe block covering: `ALL_STATE_DIRNAMES` membership; `isExplicitOpenClawHomeStateDir` true/false matrix; `resolveStateDir` + `resolveDefaultConfigCandidates` for `.openclaw` and `.clawdbot`; non-state-dir `OPENCLAW_HOME` preserves existing append behavior; implicit `$HOME=~/.openclaw` (no `OPENCLAW_HOME`) keeps the documented parent-home convention.
- `src/utils.test.ts`: add 3 new regression cases covering `resolveConfigDir` for `.openclaw`, `.clawdbot`, and the non-state-dir `OPENCLAW_HOME` regression check.

## Reproduction (before fix)

Source-reproducible against current `upstream/main` (`12e5876903`):

~~~text
$ node --import tsx ./smoke-before-fix.mjs
OPENCLAW_HOME=/home/user/.openclaw
  resolveStateDir   -> /home/user/.openclaw/.openclaw   (BUG: nested)
  resolveConfigDir  -> /home/user/.openclaw/.openclaw   (BUG: nested)
  candidates[0]     -> /home/user/.openclaw/.openclaw/openclaw.json   (BUG: writes shadow file)
~~~

## Verification (after fix)

~~~text
$ node --import tsx ./smoke-45765.mjs
PASS: OPENCLAW_HOME=/home/user/.openclaw (#45765 case)
  resolveStateDir       -> /home/user/.openclaw           (fixed; no nesting)
  resolveConfigDir      -> /home/user/.openclaw           (fixed; no nesting)
  candidates[0]         -> /home/user/.openclaw/openclaw.json   (writes the canonical path)
  no nested .openclaw   OK
PASS: OPENCLAW_HOME=/home/user/.clawdbot (legacy)
  resolveStateDir       -> /home/user/.clawdbot
  resolveConfigDir      -> /home/user/.clawdbot
  candidates[0]         -> /home/user/.clawdbot/openclaw.json
  no nested .openclaw   OK
PASS: OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw-home (non-state-dir; existing behavior must remain)
  resolveStateDir       -> /srv/openclaw-home/.openclaw   (unchanged; parent-home convention preserved)
  resolveConfigDir      -> /srv/openclaw-home/.openclaw
  candidates[0]         -> /srv/openclaw-home/.openclaw/openclaw.json
  no nested .openclaw   OK

RESULTS: 3 pass / 0 fail / 3 total
~~~

## Test

- `pnpm test src/config/paths.test.ts` — 29 passed (incl. 6 new `OPENCLAW_HOME nesting guard` regression cases)
- `pnpm test src/utils.test.ts` — 18 passed (incl. 3 new `OPENCLAW_HOME nesting guard` regression cases)
- `pnpm test src/infra/home-dir.test.ts` — 27 passed (no regressions in the underlying home-dir resolver)
- `pnpm tsgo:core` — passed (production typecheck clean)

## Notes

- Scope: 4 files, +140/-2 LOC. Production surface is the 2 resolvers plus the new exported helper and constant in `src/config/paths.ts`; the rest is the expanded regression test coverage.
- Compatibility: preserved across all existing entry points.
  - `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` / `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` explicit overrides take priority before the guard (existing `resolveStateDir` / `resolveConfigDir` early returns are unchanged).
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw-home` and any other non-state-dir basename still resolves to `/srv/openclaw-home/.openclaw` — the existing `"uses OPENCLAW_HOME for default state/config locations"` test (`paths.test.ts:151`) and `"prefers OPENCLAW_HOME over HOME for default state/config locations"` test (`paths.test.ts:158`) continue to pass without modification.
  - Implicit `$HOME=~/.openclaw` (no `OPENCLAW_HOME` set) keeps the documented `$HOME/.openclaw` convention — the guard short-circuits only when `env.OPENCLAW_HOME?.trim()` is non-empty.
  - Tilde-expanded explicit homes (`OPENCLAW_HOME=~/.openclaw`) work because `resolveEffectiveHomeDir` already runs through `expandHomePrefix` + `path.resolve` before the guard sees the resolved absolute path.
  - Symlinks: `path.basename(resolvedHome)` operates on the symlink name, not its target, so the guard matches the operator's chosen path name. This is the right semantic ("what did the user ask for?") and matches the analysis already in the prior closed attempt #45793.
- This is a narrower replacement for the closed-stale #45793 (also from a separate contributor, technically correct, lost to no maintainer engagement). The shape is the same; the new test coverage explicitly pins the implicit-`HOME` case so the guard cannot accidentally widen.
- Out-of-scope: `OPENCLAW_HOME` poisoning (already handled by `normalize()` in `src/infra/home-dir.ts:4`) and `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/path/.openclaw` style explicit-state-dir overrides (those already short-circuit before the guard at line 65).

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Onboarding with `OPENCLAW_HOME=~/.openclaw` writes the config file to the wrong place (`~/.openclaw/.openclaw/openclaw.json`) and produces a nested directory. After the fix, the config is written to the canonical `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` location.
- Real environment tested: Local checkout at `../openclaw-45765` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14 with the patched `src/config/paths.ts` and `src/utils.ts`. Smoke run exercises the actual production `resolveStateDir`, `resolveConfigDir`, and `resolveDefaultConfigCandidates` exports through `node --import tsx`, no test runner, no mocks. PR head: `44b9b82478`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `git checkout fix/openclaw-home-state-dir-nesting && pnpm install`
  - `pnpm test src/config/paths.test.ts` (29 passed)
  - `pnpm test src/utils.test.ts` (18 passed)
  - `pnpm test src/infra/home-dir.test.ts` (27 passed)
  - `pnpm tsgo:core` (EXIT=0)
  - `node --import tsx ./smoke-45765.mjs` against the three observed shapes
- Evidence after fix:
~~~text
PR head 44b9b82478
$ pnpm test src/config/paths.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  29 passed (29)
   Duration  1.96s

$ pnpm test src/utils.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
   Duration  1.97s

$ pnpm test src/infra/home-dir.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  27 passed (27)
   Duration  331ms

$ pnpm tsgo:core
EXIT=0
~~~
- Observed result after fix: with `OPENCLAW_HOME=~/.openclaw` set, `resolveStateDir` / `resolveConfigDir` both return the explicit home directly and `resolveDefaultConfigCandidates` lists `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` as the canonical first candidate — no `~/.openclaw/.openclaw/...` candidate is produced anywhere in the list. With `OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw-home` (non-state-dir basename), behavior is unchanged: `~/.openclaw` is still appended.
- What was not tested: a live `openclaw onboard` wizard run on Linux Kylin V10 / aarch64 (the reporter's environment). The behavior is source-reproducible from the patched production code through `node --import tsx` and is unit-covered by the new regression tests; a live onboarding run would only exercise the same call chain.

Closes #45765

target.title: fix(codex): quarantine unsupported dynamic tool schemas
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden Codex dynamic-tool registration so malformed runtime tool schemas are diagnosed and quarantined instead of failing assistant turn startup.
- Validate nested JSON Schema shape hazards that Codex rejects, including malformed schema maps, schema arrays, optional schema-valued keywords, `required`, property dependencies, and dynamic schema keywords.
- Preserve valid current-turn executable tools when only stale registered metadata for the same name is invalid.
- Inline local `$ref` schemas under MCP/tool-schema dependency and additional-item keywords so `$defs` are not stripped while dangling refs remain.
- Keep MCP loopback `tools/list` alive when a tool schema union contains malformed property-schema variants; malformed variants are ignored while later valid variants can still define the property.
- Preserve optionality when MCP loopback union schemas include boolean `true` branches instead of over-tightening merged `required` fields.
- Prefer later usable MCP union property schemas over earlier boolean `false` property variants so `tools/list` does not forbid valid arguments.
- Clean Gemini provider compatibility schemas inside schema-valued dependency and conditional branches so inspection and normalization agree.
- Clean shared provider compatibility schemas inside schema-valued dependency, conditional, tuple, and additional-item branches so xAI-style inspection and normalization agree.
- Make plugin tool descriptor cache keys stable and resilient to non-JSON-safe ambient tool context metadata.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible `defineToolPlugin` config and tool parameter schemas at the SDK boundary before they can enter metadata/runtime registration.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin factory tool parameter schemas before they enter shared runtime tool lists or the descriptor cache.
- Validate tool-plugin authoring metadata read from the SDK symbol so forged entries cannot feed cyclic/BigInt schemas into manifest generation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin config schema validator inputs before recursive schema walking, cache fingerprinting, or TypeBox compilation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible bundle-MCP draft-2020-12 schemas before recursive schema walking or TypeBox compilation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin config schema metadata before recursive normalization or metadata exposure.
- Omit non-JSON-compatible extension config schemas from merged config schema responses before clone/merge paths can throw.
- Reuse one shared JSON-compatibility walker across SDK and runtime plugin boundaries.
- Keep test fixtures synthetic (`fuzzplugin`/`fuzz_move_*`) instead of naming the reported third-party plugin.
- Move plugin tool metadata storage into a lightweight module so schema diagnostics can name plugin/tool ownership without importing heavier plugin runtime code.
- Out of scope: changing global plugin materialization policy or rejecting valid non-Codex schemas outside the Codex/doctor projection path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

No linked closing issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

No linked related issue.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Maintainer-directed hardening/debugging work.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: malformed runtime plugin tool schemas can make Codex reject dynamic tool registration before an assistant turn produces content.
- Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux runners, provider `aws`, using the branch over current `origin/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-tools.schema.test.ts src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts`

Yes. Malformed Codex dynamic tools are now disabled instead of being advertised or crashing registration.

Overly strict schema-shape validation could disable a valid runtime tool schema.

How is that risk mitigated?

The projection preserves valid JSON Schema forms covered by review, including boolean schemas, tuple `items`, `dependencies` string arrays, and schema-valued `additionalItems`; focused tests and changed-gate proof cover the touched surfaces.

Local branch autoreview findings were addressed before opening: property dependencies, `additionalItems`, current executable tool preservation, tuple `items`, null schema keyword diagnostics, metadata import weight, and Codex-specific projection scoping.

target.title: fix: refuse local tsgo under host pressure
target.body:
## Summary
- refuse local native type-aware checks by default on developer/agent hosts
- cover `tsgo`, type-aware `oxlint`, and sharded type-aware `oxlint`
- cover direct raw `tsgo` helper scripts: extension package boundary, core boundary, and tsgo profiling
- keep metadata commands cheap and unblocked (`--help`, `--version`, etc.)
- keep CI/GitHub Actions unblocked so broad native checks run online instead of on constrained hosts
- preserve explicit break-glass overrides for local runs
- keep pressure refusal and effective heavy-check temp-dir checks from the earlier patch
- rebase onto current `main` and preserve upstream serial shard support

## Why
A constrained Raspberry Pi/agent host repeatedly became unstable when native type-aware checks (`tsgo`/`tsgolint` via type-aware oxlint) ran locally. Serialization and throttling reduce concurrency, but they still let the same memory-hungry native check run on the same fragile host. That is the wrong failure mode.

This PR now makes the safer default explicit: local wrappers and repo helper scripts refuse native type-aware checks unless the operator opts in. CI, Blacksmith/Testbox, or another remote worker should own those broad checks.

## Behavior
Local native type-aware checks refuse by default when local heavy-check policy is active and mode is not `full`:

- `scripts/run-tsgo.mjs`
- `scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` when it runs type-aware oxlint, including explicit file-target runs
- `scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs` before artifact prep or child shard spawning
- `scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs`
- `scripts/check-tsgo-core-boundary.mjs`
- `scripts/profile-tsgo.mjs`

Bypasses / allowed cases:

- GitHub Actions / CI are not blocked
- metadata-only commands are not blocked
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=full` allows the local run
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1` allows the local run
- `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1` allows the local run
- child processes already covered by a parent heavy-check lock can continue without self-deadlock

Existing pressure checks still refuse local heavy checks when headroom is too low:

- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MIN_MEM_AVAILABLE_BYTES` defaults to `1.5 GiB`
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MIN_TMP_AVAILABLE_BYTES` defaults to `1 GiB`
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MAX_TMP_USED_PERCENT` defaults to `80`

## GitHub CI note
GitHub CI means these checks run online on GitHub/runner machines after pushing the branch, not on the local Raspberry Pi. The PR gets pass/fail status from those remote runners.

## Test Plan
Final head: `ce15395324be19fd9a07db68a8e4c7c4fe4c35e2`

Local checks intentionally stayed narrow and refusal-only. Broad lint/test/type-aware gates are left to GitHub CI.

Narrow local syntax/refusal probes run after the latest patch:

- `node --check scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs`
- `node --check scripts/profile-tsgo.mjs`
- `node --check scripts/check-tsgo-core-boundary.mjs`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled node scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs --mode=compile`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled node scripts/profile-tsgo.mjs core --json`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled node scripts/check-tsgo-core-boundary.mjs`

Previously passed locally before this direct-script follow-up:

- syntax checks for `run-tsgo`, `run-oxlint`, `run-oxlint-shards`, and local heavy-check runtime
- scoped formatter/lint checks on touched wrapper files
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm test test/scripts/local-heavy-check-runtime.test.ts test/scripts/run-oxlint.test.ts --run`
- result: `Test Files 2 passed`, `Tests 34 passed`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Local OpenClaw script entry points that can start native `tsgo`/`tsgolint` now refuse on developer/agent hosts by default, while sharded oxlint metadata commands (`--help`, `--version`, etc.) delegate directly without parent artifact preparation, lock acquisition, shard spawning, or heavy temp-dir creation.
- **Real environment tested:** Raspberry Pi OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/local-tsgo-pressure-guard`, head `00540345e8e1`, rebased onto `origin/main` `c4b9f54b4628`; local env explicitly removed `CI`/`GITHUB_ACTIONS` for refusal probes.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
git diff --check

env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE= OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAVY_CHECK_TMPDIR=.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs

env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE= OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAVY_CHECK_TMPDIR=.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --help

test ! -e .artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp

env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE= OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAVY_CHECK_TMPDIR=.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs scripts/run-tsgo.mjs

env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=full OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MIN_MEM_AVAILABLE_BYTES=999999999999999 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAVY_CHECK_TMPDIR=.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs --project config/tsconfig/root.json
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
HEAD=00540345e8e1
git diff --check: exit=0

[default sharded refusal]
exit=1
Refusing to start sharded type-aware oxlint on this local host.
Native type-aware checks can exhaust constrained developer/agent machines; run them in GitHub CI, Blacksmith/Testbox, or another remote worker instead.
Arguments: <default project run>
If you really mean to run this locally, rerun with OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1 or OPENCLAW_ALLOW_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1.

[metadata delegation / no parent temp dir]
exit=0
Usage: [-c=<./.oxlintrc.json>] [PATH]...
Basic Configuration
    -c, --config=<./.oxlintrc.json>  Oxlint configuration file

test ! -e .artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp: exit=0

[explicit file-target delegation]
exit=1
Refusing to start type-aware oxlint on this local host.
Native type-aware checks can exhaust constrained developer/agent machines; run them in GitHub CI, Blacksmith/Testbox, or another remote worker instead.
Arguments: scripts/run-tsgo.mjs --type-aware --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.json --report-unused-disable-directives-severity error --threads=1
If you really mean to run this locally, rerun with OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1 or OPENCLAW_ALLOW_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1.

[tsgo pressure refusal]
exit=1
Refusing to start a local heavy check because this host is already under pressure:
- MemAvailable 5.4 GiB is below 909 TiB
Wait for pressure to clear, free temp space, or rerun with OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1 if you really mean it.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The current head refuses the default sharded type-aware oxlint run locally before any shard work; metadata-only `--help` delegates directly and leaves `.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp` absent; an explicit file target delegates to the narrow oxlint wrapper and refuses there instead of spawning the sharded parent path; `run-tsgo` refuses under simulated host pressure before native type-aware work starts.
- **What was not tested:** Broad local CI was intentionally not run on the Raspberry Pi. Full lint/test/type-aware validation is delegated to GitHub CI/remote runners.
- **What changed because of the evidence:** The proof now covers current head `00540345e8e1` and the latest sharded oxlint refusal/setup behavior that ClawSweeper flagged as stale.

commands.review_followup: A maintainer or contributor needs to fix the stale e2e expectation and provide real behavior proof; ClawSweeper should not repair-marker a PR whose merge gate includes contributor-side live proof.

target.title: fix(plugin-state): evict current namespace on plugin row cap
target.body:
## Summary

- Make the plugin-state 1000-row per-plugin ceiling shed rows from the namespace that just grew before failing the write.
- Keep sibling namespaces protected; if the current namespace cannot free enough old rows, the write still fails atomically.
- Add regression coverage for the Telegram shape that failed in production: message-cache near its limit plus topic-name-cache rows.

## Verification

- `PATH=/home/clawuser/.openclaw/tools/node-v22.22.0/bin:$PATH OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.test.ts --reporter=dot --passWithNoTests=false`
- `PATH=/home/clawuser/.openclaw/tools/node-v22.22.0/bin:$PATH pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.sqlite.ts src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `PATH=/home/clawuser/.openclaw/tools/node-v22.22.0/bin:$PATH pnpm tsgo:core`
- `PATH=/home/clawuser/.openclaw/tools/node-v22.22.0/bin:$PATH pnpm tsgo:test:src`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram plugin state reached 1000 live rows (message-cache plus topic-name-cache), so a new topic/message cache write threw `PluginStateStoreError` and surfaced as a generic Telegram processing failure.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw plugin-state SQLite tests using the same row distribution shape: 989 `telegram.message-cache` rows plus 11 `telegram.topic-name-cache` rows.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.test.ts --reporter=dot --passWithNoTests=false` with Node 22.22.0 on branch `fix/plugin-state-current-namespace-overflow`.

Evidence after fix: The regression test registers a new message-cache row at total plugin count 1000, evicts the oldest message-cache row, preserves the topic-name-cache row, and passes in the full plugin-state test file (`23 passed`).

Observed result after fix: The current namespace frees old rows before the per-plugin ceiling throws, so Telegram message-cache growth no longer fails only because topic-name-cache is also using rows.

What was not tested: A live gateway rebuilt on this PR head; production was manually unblocked by trimming old Telegram message-cache rows before this patch.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2A conformance harness
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the regression shield. It turns the recurring channel pain into shared conformance: signed inbound webhooks, normalization, dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff, payload/live-preview delivery requests, /verbose channelData preservation, and explicit listed-provider gating. - Head: `3f295c3742` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `3f295c3742` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `3f295c3742` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: fix(plugins): retain plugin tool registry after replacement
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: cached descriptor-backed plugin tools can lose their runtime registry after the active registry is replaced, then fail with `plugin tool runtime missing`.
- Why it matters: #80847 reports contracted plugin tools becoming unavailable in sub-agent sessions after a few successful calls, with current-main confirmation of this resolver path.
- What changed: when a forced cold tool-discovery load finds the scoped plugin tools, keep that registry active so later cached descriptor executions reuse the live runtime instead of falling back to an incomplete replacement registry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not broaden plugin allowlists, tool availability, or normal plugin loading; it only retains a successful scoped tool-discovery registry.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80847
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: cached plugin tool descriptors continue executing after active runtime registry replacement instead of failing with `plugin tool runtime missing`.
- Real environment tested: Linux x86_64 dev executor, Node v22.22.2, local OpenClaw runtime harness with a real config-path plugin loaded through `resolvePluginTools()`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx --input-type=module` local runtime harness: create a config-path plugin with `contracts.tools`, resolve/capture cached plugin descriptors, replace the active registry with an incomplete registry for the same plugin id plus an unrelated live tool, then execute the cached descriptor twice.
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts --testNamePattern 'retains cold-loaded plugin tools'`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/active-runtime-registry.test.ts src/plugins/runtime.channel-pin.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the local OpenClaw runtime harness shows the cached descriptor executing twice after incomplete active registry replacement while unrelated active registry tools survive; targeted regression tests, related plugin registry tests, and `pnpm check:changed` also passed locally.

  Live terminal output from a local OpenClaw runtime harness after this patch:

  ```text
  openclaw #80847 local runtime proof
  node=v22.22.2 repo=openclaw plugin=cache-live-proof tool=cache_live_tool
  plugin_file=file:///tmp/openclaw-80847-live-DR2not/plugin/index.cjs
  resolved_initial_tools=cache_live_tool
  resolved_cached_tools=cache_live_tool
  active_registry_after_replacement_tools=1
  active_registry_after_replacement_owners=unrelated-live
  execute_after_replacement_1=live-runtime-ok-1
  active_registry_after_first_execute_tools=1
  active_registry_after_first_execute_owners=unrelated-live
  execute_after_replacement_2=live-runtime-ok-2
  active_registry_after_second_execute_tools=1
  active_registry_after_second_execute_owners=unrelated-live
  result=PASS cached descriptor survived incomplete active registry replacement without replacing unrelated active registry tools
  ```
- Observed result after fix: first cached descriptor execution returned `live-runtime-ok-1`; the unrelated active registry owner remained `unrelated-live` after both executions; the second execution returned `live-runtime-ok-2` using the retained scoped recovery registry without replacing global active state.
- What was not tested: the reporter's private `lossless-claw` plugin and live five-minute sub-agent archival loop were not available here. Crabbox CLI was installed locally, but remote AWS Crabbox proof was blocked by missing local Crabbox/AWS auth (`broker=- auth=missing`; `crabbox doctor` failed at AWS credentials), so no remote Crabbox run is claimed.
- Before evidence: the new regression failed before the fix with `plugin tool runtime unavailable (cache-lifecycle-test): cached_lifecycle_tool` on the second cached descriptor execution.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `resolvePluginToolRegistry()` forced a cold standalone load when an active/channel registry existed but did not cover the requested plugin tools, but that successful cold-loaded registry was not retained for later cached descriptor executions.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing cached descriptor tests did not simulate active registry replacement followed by repeated cached tool execution.
- Contributing context (if known): recent registry compatibility hardening made partial registry reuse stricter, which is correct, but the recovery cold-load path was ephemeral.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: cached plugin tool descriptors execute successfully after the active registry is replaced by an incomplete registry for the same plugin id.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it directly exercises the resolver/cached descriptor path that throws the reported error without needing the reporter's private plugin.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Contracted plugin tools remain available across registry replacement/sub-agent lifecycle changes instead of intermittently failing until gateway restart.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
cached descriptor -> incomplete active registry -> one-off cold load -> later execution fails

After:
cached descriptor -> incomplete active registry -> scoped cold load retained -> later execution succeeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux x86_64
- Runtime/container: local Node/Vitest checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): plugin tool runtime
- Relevant config (redacted): default test plugin config

### Steps

1. Resolve plugin tools once to seed cached descriptors.
2. Replace the active registry with an incomplete registry that still declares the same plugin id.
3. Execute the cached descriptor tool twice.

### Expected

- Both executions succeed; the first recovery cold-load is retained for the second execution.

### Actual

- Before this patch, the second execution rejected with `plugin tool runtime unavailable`.
- After this patch, both executions resolve successfully.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: local OpenClaw runtime harness with a real config-path plugin, RED/GREEN regression, related plugin registry tests, formatting, staged secret scan, changed gate.
- Edge cases checked: compatible active registry reuse, pinned channel registry behavior, active runtime registry filtering.
- What you did **not** verify: private reporter plugin, live sub-agent archival timing, remote Crabbox proof due local auth blocker.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: retaining a recovery registry could accidentally replace broader active/channel/http runtime state.
  - Mitigation: retention is descriptor-scoped and stored outside global active state; the regression now verifies unrelated active registry tools survive recovery execution.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 3 official channel capability matrix
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR prevents the long tail from becoming a new pile of native branches. It expresses official app/API and regional plugin platforms as broker aliases and capability rows first, including WeChat/Weixin, and it enforces declared delivery capabilities before outbound handoff. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 3 - Official/app platform capability matrix - Role: Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. - Head: `7bff662dee` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `7bff662dee` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 3 official/app channel capability matrix for Channel Broker. - Keeps the work at the broker contract/catalog layer: no native channel migration defaults change in this PR. - Stacks on Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86113 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86144, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds canonical broker platform aliases while keeping `platform: string` open for provider-owned long-tail targets: - `teams` / `msteams` -> `microsoft-teams` - `googlechat` -> `google-chat` - `qq` -> `qqbot` - Aligns `channels.channel-broker.*.capabilities` config with the SDK capability shape: - `delivery` - `live` - `receive` - `native` - Adds focused proof for Microsoft Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, LINE, Feishu, QQ bot, Zalo, Mattermost, Nextcloud Talk, Twitch, IRC, Nostr, Tlon, and Synology Chat. - Documents the Phase 3 platform matrix and SDK capability shape. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Official/app channel aliases normalize through the broker catalog, and Phase 3 platforms route through provider-owned broker requests without enabling native migration defaults. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:teams:19:meeting-channel"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "teams-alias", "messageId": "native-teams-alias", "platform": "microsoft-teams", "conversation": { "id": "19:meeting-channel", "type": "channel" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path canonicalized the Teams alias to `microsoft-teams` and produced a provider-owned broker request with no platform-specific native extension branch. - **What was not tested:** Live Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, regional, or self-hosted provider traffic; this PR proves the broker catalog/schema/conformance layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` Note: `pnpm exec markdownlint-cli2 ...` was unavailable in the local workspace, so the documented `pnpm dlx` path was used. It linted the docs glob cleanly. ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - This PR does not move native routing defaults for any Phase 3 channel.
